message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic has placed unprecedented stress on health systems across the world, and reliable estimates of risk for adverse hospital outcomes are needed.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To quantify admission laboratory and comorbidity features associated with critical illness and mortality risk across 6 Eastern Massachusetts hospitals.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Retrospective cohort study using hospital course, prior diagnoses, and laboratory values.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Emergency department and inpatient settings from 2 academic medical centers and 4 community hospitals.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>All individuals with hospital admission and positive severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) by PCR testing across these 6 hospitals through June 5, 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposure</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome Measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Severe illness defined by ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, or death.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Among 2,511 hospitalized individuals who tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 (of whom 50.9% were male, 53.9% white, and 27.0% Hispanic, with mean age 62.6 years), 215 (8.6%) were admitted to the ICU, 164 (6.5%) required mechanical ventilation, and 292 (11.6%) died. L1-regression models developed in 3 of these hospitals yielded area under ROC curve (AUC) of 0.807 for severe illness and 0.847 for mortality in the 3 held-out hospitals. In total, 212/292 (78%) of the deaths occurred in the highest-risk mortality quintile.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this cohort, specific admission laboratory studies in concert with sociodemographic features and prior diagnosis facilitated risk stratification among individuals hospitalized for COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>1R56MH115187-01</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Trial Registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>None</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key Points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>How well can sociodemographic features, laboratory values, and comorbidities of individuals hospitalized with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Eastern Massachusetts through June 5, 2020 predict severe illness course?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>In this cohort study of 2,511 hospitalized individuals positive for severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) by PCR who were admitted to one of six hospitals, 215 (8.6%) were admitted to the ICU, 164 (6.5%) required mechanical ventilation, and 292 (11.6%) died. In a risk prediction model, 212 (78%) deaths occurred in the top mortality-risk quintile.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Simple prediction models may assist in risk stratification among hospitalized COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Laboratory findings associated with severe illness and mortality among hospitalized individuals with coronavirus disease 2019 in Eastern Massachusetts

reference.unstructured: Consortium for Clinical Characterization of Covid 19 by EHR (4CE). i2b2 tranSMART Foundation. [Published April 5, 2020]. Accessed April 19, 2020. https://transmartfoundation.org/covid-19-community-project/

reference.article-title: Calculating the benefits of a Research Patient Data Repository

reference.unstructured: Quality A for HR and. HCUP-US Tools & Software Page. Clinical Classifications Software (CCS) for ICD-9-CM. https://www.hcup-us.ahrq.gov/tools_software.jsp

reference.article-title: Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso
reference.journal-title: J R Stat Soc Ser B Methodol
